summary: DeVault argues that the Free Software Foundation's pedantic insistence on terminology — correcting 'open source' to 'free software,' 'Linux' to 'GNU/Linux,' etc. — alienates potential allies who already agree with the underlying ideas. He contends that the FSF's linguistic pedantry pushes people away from the movement rather than growing it, and that the crucial distinction is really about copyleft, not the free/open-source label. He advocates using whatever terms the audience already knows while preaching free software ideals, focusing energy on systemic goals like open-source in education and right to repair rather than policing vocabulary.
key_points: Free software and open-source software are practically identical — licenses qualifying for one but not the other are exceptionally rare
Pedantic corrections alienate people who already agree with you on the ideas, trading movement growth for self-satisfaction
The FSF's conflation of 'free software' advocacy with copyleft advocacy confuses listeners and undermines both messages
The movement should attack systems and institutions that imposed proprietary software, not correct their victims' vocabulary
Language policing matters when companies appropriate open-source terminology to disguise proprietary licenses, but not when allies use slightly different words for the same ideas
money_quotes: The audience already agrees with the speaker on the ideas, but this becomes less so with every word.
The speaker gains the self-satisfaction that comes with demonstrating that you're smarter than someone else, but the cost is pushing that person away from the very ideals you're trying to clarify.
This approach doesn't help the movement, it's just being a dick.
The ideas are what matters, the goal is to get people thinking on your wavelength. If they hang around long enough, they'll start using your words, too. That's how language works.

If your goal is to strengthen the resolve and unity of people who already agree with you by appealing to tribalism, then this approach is effective - but remember that it strengthens the opposing tribes, too.
Attack the systems and individuals who brought about the circumstances that frustrate your movement, but don't attack their victims.
Why is 'get everyone to say libre instead of open-source' one of our goals instead?
When someone has the wrong words but the right ideas, it's not a big deal. When someone has the wrong ideas and is appropriating the words to support them, that's a problem.
key_insight: Winning people to free software ideals requires meeting them where they are linguistically rather than alienating potential allies with pedantic corrections that prioritize tribal signaling over movement growth.
